data.title: Kforce: Structural Grower With Tailwinds Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Since the middle of 2021, Kforce Inc.'s (NYSE:KFRC) stock has done about nothing, down ~12% over the past roughly 3 years. I’ve always been intrigued by the business from afar (as I feel like technology labor is perpetually a shortage), so I wanted to follow up to see if there’s any opportunity. Summarily, I do think their fundamental outlook is positive, and they’ll grow over time, but I’m just not sure today’s price (~$51) offers that great of a reward from an IRR perspective to justify an investment given other opportunities out there.

Sales: Structural Grower

Consolidated sales came in at ~$344M in Q4, down just about 3% from the $353M in sales they posted in last year’s Q4. For the full year, sales declined just over 8% to $1.4B, which implicitly illustrates some of the recent stability they’ve seen. Breaking out their Q4 results by segment, both Technology and FA declined low-single-digit year-over-year - there wasn’t any dichotomy here. And the same story goes for the Flex versus Direct Hire trends - both were down about the same amount.

With those trends now in mind, let’s dig in starting with their Tech segment. They note that “[o]verall, average bill rates in our technology business of $90 were stable again in the fourth quarter and in fact, have been stable for more than two years.” (We can confirm this by taking their Flex revenue and dividing it by the total hours worked/billed.) So, from a pricing perspective, there was no change here impacting revenue - the decline in sales was a result of reduced hours worked (volume).

Splitting this out, there are sorts of two separate trends in the Tech segment volume-wise. As they’ve been recently calling out, they have a growing “consulting” business. This has necessitated KFRC adding new personnel for that business specifically, but it’s also probably utilizing existing consultants. But either way, these consulting engagements were higher, while the softness was generated by their traditional staffing:

Holding aside consulting engagements, amid today’s economic environment, it’s really not all that crazy to expect companies to have reduced their discretionary spend, or more specifically, discretionary projects. This is clearly evidenced by not only reduced demand across many businesses, but the responsive layoffs that we’ve seen many large businesses institute as well. And for context, KFRC’s mix of customers skews to “financial and business services, communications, insurance, retail and technology,” for which I think the aforementioned layoffs are representative of what their clients then have likely seen. And indeed, KFRC’s team called out this trend on the call with “clients looking at their budgets.”

So, I do believe there’s some argument to be made that they’ve seen clients defer projects that have reduced the total hours worked. And qualitatively, it would make sense as not every project is a cost-cutting one. Instead, a lot of these are instead growth-oriented, forward-facing projects, installing new systems, software, and tools designed to grow the business. So, when a period arises when it’s critical to conserve cash - as today’s evidence suggests - it should be the case that clients indeed reduce project spending where they can.

From a market share perspective, while this is hard to fully confirm, it does look quite unlikely that they’ve lost market share. From a real-time perspective, Robert Half Inc. (RHI) - a competitor to KFRC - saw Q4 sales down about 6%. And we can also see that their “Technology” and “Finance and Accounting” contract labor sales are also down in that territory, which is more comparable to KFRC. TrueBlue, Inc. (TBI) has a slightly different mix and is seemingly going through some idiosyncratic changes, but their sales were down 19% in Q3. So, it’s clearly not just KFRC seeing reduced demand, supporting the idea that these are market-driven declines, particularly given that KFRC’s declines are a little more muted than those players.

Qualitatively too, nothing’s really changed from an offering perspective that might justify a change in market share. Price is unchanged as noted above, and then in terms of consultant supply/availability, I’m not seeing or hearing anything here which would result in a business seeking talent elsewhere. Furthermore, with demand lower, new entrants are also an unlikely explanation for anything. And they pretty much confirmed this too, citing that they “haven't really heard of any major competitors disruptions there.” Furthermore, “[a]s we go through any tougher times, we see a lot of local and/or regional entities that are not well capitalized, that might have a portfolio. They're in many instances, one client receivable going bad away from out of business.”

Looking ahead, today's Tech sales of ~$317M probably amount to ~$1.28B on an annualized basis (there’s not too much seasonality here). We’ll see what happens market-wise as we go forward, but it’s certainly possible we see some residual macro headwinds in 2025 as businesses continue to rationalize their spend. But per current sales trends being in the low-single-digit area, it’s clearly not too impactful, so I’m thinking about sales potentially being flat in 2025, if not marginally higher. They, too, note that they’ve “continued to experience stability in our business other than normal seasonality at the end of the year as clients naturally conclude projects and begin to reassess budgets and technology priorities in the new year.”

Structurally speaking though, I’m basically concluding that what we’re seeing year-over-year isn’t a perpetual trend and that KFRC can grow over time like they’ve shown historically. For one, as noted at the beginning, management has been hinting at the consulting-oriented projects being a nice growth contributor going forward with (seemingly) untapped demand that they’re now covering. They’re devoting more resources to building this team out, so I’d expect growth over time here.

On the non-consulting side though, while the market may soften a little further in 2025, the number of projects will continue to increase over time alongside general economic growth. For the technical areas they target, I struggle to believe we’re anywhere near full penetration in terms of getting full use out of those skills, i.e., I don’t think all potential businesses are fully utilizing all possible use cases of their talent pool - there are likely untapped projects that, for competitive reasons, many businesses today will eventually need to address and in increasing numbers, as they’ve shown historically.

Share-wise, it’s actually been the case historically that they’ve been winning share. They’ve touched on how vendor consolidation from larger, enterprise organizations has benefited them as they’ve been one to remain in the consolidated pack, picking up the business from terminated vendors. I don’t have data to quantify just how beneficial this can be going forward, but it is nonetheless telling about their competitive position.

That is, there appear to be some scale advantages that have grown over time, but specifically for large organizations, not so much small ones. There appears to be a larger value placed on reliability, compliance, and national delivery that small staffing agencies, by definition, can’t provide. But from both a new entrant and intra-market competition perspective, both of these appear normalized today - I don’t see where these may cause some disruption going forward. KFRC continues to invest their offering at a solid clip, and history shows that (1) they’ve done this successfully and (2) competitors at large have behaved decently and rationally.

The overarching risk is that clients decide to prioritize more in-house labor, but I think the trend to incrementally outsource projects is decently sticky/will continue. From what I can gather as an outsider, the trend is seemingly predicated on economics - i.e., “While I can house the talent internally because the project types are changing so rapidly, it really makes more sense to contract out to remain more flexible.” So, I struggle to envision a world where companies incrementally start either (1) prioritizing full-time labor - where they even can - or (2) seeing a reduction in the number of technology-based projects.

With all of this in mind then, over time, this should be a GDP-plus business given the secular technological trends. So, I’m modeling 4% long-term sales growth, which may be too light for the interim (beyond FY25) for which they could maybe grow 5%+. However, for FY25, I’m assuming 0% growth in FY25, so the growth is from an FY25 sales base of ~$1.28B.

I’m not going to extensively cover their FA business given its immaterial size. I’m modeling no growth given their desire to move away from this segment - i.e., seemingly not proactively going after incremental customers or devoting full attention to existing ones. However, there is probably some base of sales that is going to carry on for some time given that, I’d imagine, some customers are likely pulling FA talent from KFRC given the convenience of already utilizing their Tech consultants.

Today’s FA sales of $26.5M put them in the $110M territory on an annualized basis. Thus, on a consolidated basis, I’m assuming FY25 sales of $1.39B thereabouts. That can probably grow higher in the interim, but over time, I’m assuming a roughly 3.5% blended long-term sales growth.

Margins: Untapped Operating Leverage

Q4 gross margins came in at 27%, down 30 bps from 27.3% posted last year. By segment, their Technology gross margins were flat at 26.1% year-over-year, while their FA gross margins declined ~150 bps to 37.5% from 39% posted last year, so the consolidated margin decline was FA-driven. And then from an EBITDA perspective (on a consolidated basis), they posted margins of ~6%, down about 110 bps from the ~7.1% posted in Q4 last year (EBITDA margins aren’t broken out by segment).

Regarding Tech’s flat gross margins year-over-year, we talked earlier about how bill rates were more or less flat year-over-year, so from that angle, there’s not going to be any margin impact from that. From a cost perspective too, pay rates have apparently remained stable as well, as they note that “[t]he consistent demand for highly skilled talent on both traditional staffing assignments and consulting-oriented engagements have kept bill and pay rate stable even as the overall industry trends have slowed.”

However, the trend seemingly changed in Q4 as they note “higher health care costs in the fourth quarter were offset by a slight improvement in spreads year-over-year.” So, pay rates must have actually marginally declined here, widening spreads relative to flat bill rates versus last year. There will be some mix impact, though. While part of the improved spreads was perhaps just a function of organic marketplace trends - i.e., consultants competing and willing to accept lower pay for work - part of the improvement stemmed from a mix shift with more consulting work. They noted that spreads benefited from the “evolution of our business to include a more significant amount of consulting-oriented work.” So, improved spreads were more or less offset by higher associate/consultant costs (non-pay rate COGS), producing roughly flat gross margins.

From a different angle, there’s not going to be much in the way of fixed COGS that’s going to create any material deleverage impact. The vast majority of their COGS will be their pay rates that they’re sending to the consultants (variable by nature), and their related compensation and employment costs (noted above). The internal supporting personnel costs are going to be included in SG&A. So, when I think about Tech sales being down low-single-digits year-over-year, there’s not going to be much deleverage associated with that.

For the FA segment, margins were down much more than Tech. This is not the result of bill rate declines as here too, bill rates were about flat at ~$51. However, the aforementioned spread benefits on the Tech side may not be present here and thus, not offsetting what are higher non-pay COGS here too. They also aren’t capturing the same mix shift tailwind to consulting. So, with fixed cost leverage de minimis here as well, I’m basically inferring that the cost growth had to do more with compensation and support-related costs than pay rates increasing (and reducing bill-pay spreads).

From an operating level, however, we can see that there was additional opex deleverage on top of the gross margin declines. From what I can gather, this is mostly - but not entirely - standard fixed cost deleverage. If we zoom out for a second, back in 2023, they pushed through a cost-saving initiative to save about $14M in annualized costs, or ~$3.5M quarterly, which was a function of both reduced staff (given the softening demand environment) and reduced office space. This seemingly was fully realized in Q4 of 2023, so when I think about the context here, there really shouldn’t have been much residual savings post-4Q23 that would be benefiting today versus Q4 last year.

However, there are two headwinds at play. While they can reduce some sales and delivery staff over the past 12 months (beyond the initiative), they can’t reduce all of them linearly with the sales over the past 12 months - there’s a utilization angle here which, given the reduced sales versus last year, is being realized. This is further confirmed on the recent calls with management discussing how they currently have more capacity than they need for today’s demand, although this is partly intentional as they’re hanging on to talent in anticipation of demand returning.

Furthermore, they’re also making offsetting investments to grow the team’s productivity. I don’t actually know (yet) what these “investments” are, but they specifically note that investments have been allocated to “sales” while their “delivery” resources are down 15% year-over-year. And they’re also building out a consulting team, so it’s likely mostly headcount growth on top of their back-office transformation.

So, when thinking about the opex deleverage year-over-year, there are some puts and takes at work here. But from a higher-level perspective, the general decline in operating margins had seemingly more to do with the natural deleverage from reduced utilization. Some of this was, however, clearly amplified by recent, ongoing investments they’ve been making to support the team, but lower utilization is more so the broader explanation.

Thinking about all of this going forward, should sales grow over time like I expect - even if flat in 2025 - there should be some margin growth associated with this. From a pricing perspective, I’m not expecting really any material degradation here. Actually, if demand begins to rise, this could generate a more positive price lag which might actually slightly boost gross margins. Inversely, today’s pricing stability amidst today’s demand environment is a really good sign, in my opinion.

The bigger leverage point over time, however, will be what I’ve just been discussing which is getting more leverage/productivity out of their existing sales and delivery capacity. To quantify this, their publicized target is for “double-digit” operating (not EBITDA) margins on $2B of sales.

To this end, they’re seemingly targeting 10% operating margins specifically, so relative to today’s ~4.5% GAAP operating margins on ~$1.39B of annualized sales, that would imply incremental EBITDA margins of ~20% thereabouts. Now, there is some seasonality it appears where Q4 margins are a little lower than a normalized full-year average, but it probably only puts current operating margins in the ~5% range on the $1.39B of sales, so clearly, they’re still guiding for roughly a double in margins, and incremental margins of 21%.

Is that achievable? Well, versus last year, they incurred decremental EBITDA margins of ~30%. Now, I wrote that this is likely amplified by opex investments they’ve made in the past 12 months, but it’s not totally unreasonable to think a more normalized rate would’ve indeed been around 20%. In 2022, they posted ~10% incremental EBITDA margins before posting decrementals of ~14% in 2023. And then pre-COVID, it was about the same range in the teens territory.

With that history in mind on top of the fact that we know that today’s operating profile has excess capacity and unleveraged “investments”, management might then be right in their ability to produce double-digit operating margins on $2B of sales. For context, they posted ~7% operating margins on ~$1.71B in FY22 sales, so there’s certainly room to grow over time.

To capture the full intrinsic value of this business, we do need to stretch the model a little bit further. That is, if I scale out the aforementioned sales growth rates, they’d be hitting a little over $1.7B in sales in FY30. Margin-wise, I’ll assume 18% for now to be slightly more aggressive relative to their history - 20% is too uncomfortably high for me even though it appears doable - which gets me to 7.5% operating margins. Add back another 0.5% thereabouts for D&A and 8% EBITDA margins on $1.713B in sales is what I’m modeling.

Valuation

Attractively, KFRC is a very capital-light business, so capex basically runs at a de minimis level every year in the area of ~$8-9-10M (think capitalized software costs, computers, office space, etc.). I’m modeling capex going forward at ~0.75%.

But another attractive angle on top of this is their capital allocation policy for the residual FCF. If we just look year-to-date, on top of some debt repayments, they’ve sent back all the leftover cash to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases. This is also evident over the past 3 years as well, so this isn't a near-term policy change. But inversely, they’re not spending money on M&A, and this is the part that I like. They went on to further solidify this stance on the call too, noting that M&A is quite unlikely for valuation and strategic reasons.

So, looking ahead, I’m expecting FCF to be allocated to non-M&A outlets. Putting together the model here, I’m assuming that FY30 sales/EBITDA can come in around $1.713B/$137M. If we then subtract out D&A of ~$7M (0.4% of sales), SBC of ~$17M (1% of sales), interest expense of $2M, taxes at 25%, and then capex of ~$13M (0.75% of sales), I get a bottom line free-cash-flow estimate of ~$77M.

With ~18.6M diluted shares outstanding, KFRC is currently trading at a market cap of ~$947M ($50/share). Current cash stands at less than a million, and total debt stands at just under $33M.

At this valuation, then, this is sort of a “how much do I want to stretch my valuation” situation. In other words, they’re trading at an earnings yield of ~8% on FY30 estimates right now, which only implies a roughly high-single-digit-to-~10% IRR if we assume linear cash generation in the interim and assume they can grow at GDP+ over time. Fundamentally, I do think that KFRC is going to grow over time, but I’m just not totally comfortable with the level of growth that’s needed, basically, to get the IRR above 10%. I’m not being that conservative with sales growth.

If your IRR hurdle is lower than that or if you believe they’re going to grow sales faster and capture higher margins, you could certainly justify an investment here (and given their history, I wouldn’t fault anyone). I’ll admit that while my mid-single-digit growth assumption is more than I’m generally comfortable with, they’ve posted that historically. So I could actually be understating their valuation here. But with other stocks like Franklin Covey Co. (FC) and Distribution Solutions Group, Inc. (DSGR) trading at low-double-digit IRRs, I’m not sure it makes much sense to buy KFRC versus the others.

Conclusion

All in all, I like KFRC’s business and would be happy to own them at the right price. However, I’m just not sure ~$50 is the right price. Perhaps I’m too picky here and need to pay up for their future/growth tailwinds and attractive capital allocation, but I’m just not comfortable given my opportunity cost.

data.title: Mapletree Industrial: A Hold Considering Lease Renewal Risks And Capital Allocation Moves
data.description:
My Hold rating for Mapletree Industrial Trust (OTCPK:MAPIF) (ME8U:SI) is unchanged. I evaluate the Singapore-listed REIT's capital management approach and the risks pertaining to tenancy renewal in this write-up. With my earlier May 19, 2024, article, the spotlight was on MAPIF's negatives such as asset valuation decline and positives like property sale plans.

I'm Worried About The REIT's Mid-Teens Percent Of Leases Expiring Soon

MAPIF's properties whose current lease terms will conclude in FY26 (YE March 31, 2026) make up 17% of its gross revenue as disclosed in its February 2025 presentation. My prediction is that one-third of these above-mentioned leases won't be successfully renewed. That means that it could possibly lose 5% of its GRI ("Gross Rental Income") in the near term.

The REIT's YoY topline expansion decelerated from +4.2% for the second quarter of fiscal 2025 to +2.0% in 3QFY25 (3 months ended December 2024). Its recent quarterly revenue was equivalent to a -2.7% miss according to S&P Capital IQ data. The portfolio vacancy metric for MAPIF worsened sequentially from 7.1% to 7.9% during the same time frame. The negative momentum associated with its sub-par third quarter performance might persist into the new year.

Mapletree Industrial's North American Data Center Assets

ME8U indicated in its latest presentation slides that selected US data center tenants representing 170 basis points of GRI won't be continuing with their leases upon expiry in FY 2026. Previously, MAPIF was only able to extend the lease for its San Diego data center property by one and a half years to mid-2026 (calendar year), rather than securing a new multi-year tenancy contract. These developments suggest that the downside non-renewal risks for the REIT's data center portfolio in the US are significant. It is reasonable to speculate that Mapletree could be encountering stiffer competition from other data center owners boasting newer and more sizable assets.

Another area of concern is the REIT's industrial property situated at Tai Seng, Singapore. The asset has its lease coming to an end in May this year and accounts for close to 2% of MAPIF's gross revenue. At an analyst meeting in October last year, ME8U cautioned that its rent for this property might be cut significantly following tenancy renewal. Roughly half of the Tai Seng property's existing rent was variable and linked to prior property improvement works done, which won't likely recur in the future.

But My View Of MAPIF's Scrip Dividend Plan And Portfolio Optimization Is Favorable

The REIT might be able to partially offset the unfavorable effects of lease expiries with value-enhancing capital allocation moves.

MAPIF has a scheme in place to give its shareholders a choice to take their dividends in stock. It generated SGD 8 million (9% of quarterly distributable income) of excess capital in the recent quarter thanks to this distribution reinvestment initiative. I believe it will use this spare cash to pay down its borrowings. This might translate into lower finance expenses and an expanded payout capacity for ME8U.

Separately, it can boost its future results by remaking its portfolio.

ME8U mentioned at the REIT's late 2024 analyst call that it is reviewing its US data center assets with an eye on potential restructuring actions. My opinion is that this could involve the redevelopment of certain underperforming properties or outright sales to support fresh acquisitions.

In the preceding section, I highlighted that Mapletree's property portfolio in North America was facing renewal-related challenges. It makes sense to upgrade a few of its sub-scale or older facilities to improve the chances of retaining existing tenants.

Also, it isn't good to be overly reliant on any single geographical market. As of December 31, 2024, properties in Singapore, North America, and Japan accounted for 47%, 46% and 7% of MAPIF's asset base, respectively. If you were the REIT manager, it would be sensible to diversify away from your place of listing and the US.

MAPIF's Purchase Of Japanese Data Center

In the final quarter of the calendar year 2024, ME8U broadened its presence in Japan as detailed in the chart presented above. The REIT had made a shrewd move in buying a data center in the western part of Tokyo. The majority or over 70% of the city's data center capacity is in Greater Tokyo according to third-party data cited by MAPIF in the company's presentation. This implies that the other regions like Tokyo West boast comparatively more favorable supply dynamics. The end-October M&A deal could be a stepping stone for the REIT to execute similar transactions in the area.

The Final Word

I am projecting that MAPIF's dividend per unit will decrease by -3% from SGD 0.137 for FY25 to SGD 0.133 in FY 2026. My forecast has considered the potential decline in the REIT's occupancy driven by tenancy expiries, partly negated by a decline in interest costs and an improved inorganic growth outlook.

My view is that ME8U is reasonably valued and deserving of a Hold. Its forward FY26 dividend yield is 6.5%, or just slightly above its all-time historical mean of 6.2% as per S&P Capital IQ. It is fair that the stock is offering an above-average yield to compensate for its uninspiring prospects.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SPXT: S&P 500 Ex-Tech Is Unpopular For Good Reason
data.description:
If you were ever that kid in school who everyone either picked on or ignored, you might feel a bit of kinship with ProShares S&P 500® ex-Technology ETF (NYSEARCA:SPXT). I know I do. And I only got picked on a little.

The chart below could be sung to the tune of "one of these things is not like the other," since the S&P 500 (SPY) has been propelled higher largely by the technology sector, which is represented in symbol XLK below. Just look at that asset growth over the past five years. SPY from roughly $200 billion to three times that size, and XLK from $20 billion to more than $75 billion. Some of that was the roaring stock market, but a lot of it was investors pouring assets into those funds. It may have been a self-fulfilling prophecy, but for those who participated, it was indeed fulfilling.

Oh, but there's another proverbial spec of dust on the same chart above. It is like the kid that gets picked last in gym class, or the one left out of all the cool kids' activities. I am stressing those high school analogies because I think it is the best way to understand what it has been like to be an investor in SPXT during most of this time. Sure, its assets grew from very little to over $200 million. But that's million, not billion. Nowhere near the asset-gathering success of the other two.

SPY = XLK + SPXT

Keep in mind that XLK is one of 11 sector ETFs that track the S&P 500, which itself is tracked by SPY. That means the other 10 sectors of the S&P 500 have populated SPXT.

Here's the same trio, but with total return for the past five years instead of AUM growth. Tech has more than doubled the return of non-tech. And that is why SPXT is an ETF many investors may not even know existed.

However, with a market starting to shift beneath our feet, just a bit, this might be a good time for index investors to start figuratively looking both ways before they cross the street. In other words, not just cling to tech stocks, the masters of the global stock market universe, but also consider those other 10 sectors. Tech is the biggest individual sector of SPY, but yes, there are 10 others.

Getting to know SPXT

SPXT is one of the better ETF creation ideas. But nearing its 10th anniversary, it is likely its managers could not have anticipated just how out of favor non-tech would continuously be over that time. Even when the market crashed for five weeks in early 2020 as the pandemic struck, big tech lost less. That was very different from in past bear markets. And it was just part of the best run for the tech sector versus the rest since the global financial crisis in 2008.

SPXT is cheap enough, with an expense ratio of just 0.09%. I don't normally fixate on that, but this is a true "full replication" index ETF. That means it owns all 436 stocks not part of the tech sector, allocated by each stock's market capitalization.

Concentration & turnover

Removing tech highlights other sectors

Tech is an unhealthy 36% of SPY. So when SPXT excludes that sector (other than a small portion which is probably due to tech spinoffs from non-tech stocks) we can see that financials, cyclicals, healthcare, and communications are all major players. Those four represent nearly 66% of SPXT.

While many stocks can be considered to be technology companies, SPXT bases its weightings on S&P sector guidelines, which assign each stock to only one sector. So, while a trio of tech giants (AAPL, NVDA, MSFT) are not present, Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and three other Magnificent 7 stocks are. META and GOOG/GOOGL are part of the communications sector, and TSLA and AMZN are part of consumer cyclicals. Those four of the iconic seven still make up 19% of SPXT, but that's a far less influential position compared to the full Mag 7's domination of SPY. And, MSFT, AAPL and NVDA account for a robust 40% of XLK, the tech sector ETF.

Strong recent momentum makes SPXT more competitive

The leaders within SPXT are profitable equity market giants, and they are expensive on basic valuation metrics like much of tech is. But those momentum grades indicate that there is a bit of a "catch-up" trade going on recently.

Extending the top holdings to the 25 largest, one thing that stands out is the lack of dividend yield. That's a market-wide phenomenon, since even traditionally high yielders are lower than normal, given the lift the broad market has seen over the past decade.

And even when we remove the pricey technology sector, with the exception of Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway and a pair of energy names, the PE Ratios are quite rich here.

Holdings reveal a lack of value

Looking at the full SPXT portfolio, nearly 24x earnings is not much below the full market multiple sported by SPY. And 2.5x sales is not a bargain either. Earnings growth projected in the upper single digits might help to assuage the market for a while. But to say SPXT is "undervalued" is a stretch. There's also not much yield here, though this ETF's 1.8% yield is about triple that of XLK.

Earnings growth is a potential savior, if it comes through

SPXT has competed well with SPY very recently, but that has not broken its long losing streak. On a 3-year rolling basis, it has underperformed in every single period of the past 60 data points here. So it takes a contrarian to want to step up and buy SPXT.

Indeed, 2025 has been a non-tech year so far, but not by enough to turn many heads, I suspect.

SPXT: better as a benchmark than a holding for now

Like I said above, it will take a contrarian. I am a contrarian at heart. But I'm not "that" contrarian just yet. The ideal scenario for me would be to see the whole market fall down hard, and for investors to feel so burned by big tech stocks, SPXT might make a good place to hang out while that works itself off. The dot com bubble bust was the last time we saw that play out. I'm not holding my breath on this one.

That said, I have long used SPXT as a benchmark. That is, I track it regularly, so I can get a sense as to what degree tech has been outdistancing all US equity peers. As they say in boxing, to become a champion, you have to knock out the champ. SPXT will likely get there at some point, but with every temporary "win" versus tech and thus the broader S&P 500 being so brief, it will take more than a couple of tactical rallies each year to get the dunce cap removed from this ETF, relatively and figuratively speaking.

data.title: CAVA Group: Why You Should Sell Now And Wait For A Retreat
data.description:
Research and analysis conducted by Patrik Backus

Investment Thesis

We believe CAVA Group, Inc. (NYSE:CAVA) has significant long-term potential. Ten years from now, it could become a household name. However, the stock is currently trading at an extremely high valuation—over 14x NTM EV/S, 100x NTM EV/EBITDA, and 240x NTM P/E. The reasons behind such a high valuation are due to CAVA’s meaningful upside potential and investors’ enthusiasm for growth for the next decade and more.

To properly evaluate CAVA and determine a sensible valuation range, we believe the most effective approach is to project CAVA’s upcoming growth with that of Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. (CMG) in 2004-2023, during which it had similar store counts, revenue, and revenue growth. This comparison will help gauge the potential milestones CAVA might achieve if it follows a similar trajectory.

Brief Intro on CAVA

CAVA is a fast-casual Mediterranean restaurant chain with approximately 360 locations across the United States at the end of 2024. Since its IPO in June 2023, the company has experienced rapid growth, with many describing it “the next Chipotle”

  • Founded in 2006 in Rockville, Maryland, it was initially a single full-service restaurant offering traditional Greek dishes.

  • In 2011, the founders saw the opportunity in fast-casual, given Chipotle’s success, and pivoted toward that direction. The growth took off.

  • Acquired Zoes Kitchen in 2018 for $300M. This acquisition expanded CAVA’s footprint into new markets.

  • Successful IPO in 2023. The stock surged on its first trading day to a valuation north of $4B.

  • Currently, CAVA is trading at around $140/share and has a market cap of just over $16B.

What makes CAVA intriguing?

CAVA's stock price nearly tripled in 2024, rising from $42 to $115. At its peak on December 6, 2024, it surpassed $150. Such a remarkable performance is rare in the restaurant and dining sector. If CAVA is the next Chipotle with significant growth potential ahead, it’s a stock that shouldn’t be missed and worth analyzing closely.

CAVA has a unique growth story in its early stages of being a publicly traded company. To elaborate, it is important to look at two key metrics and how they typically relate to each other, to see why CAVA is a bright spot in the fast-casual dining industry. Same-restaurant sales growth (or SRS growth) measures how much a company’s existing pool of stores grows periodically. In the third quarter of 2024, CAVA announced SRS growth of 18.1% (compared to CMG’s 6%), consisting of 12.9% traffic growth and 5.2% average check size growth. This figure raised annual company guidance/consensus expectations from 8.5-9.5% to 12.0-13.0%.

While it is not uncommon necessarily to see such figures for a fast-growing company like CAVA, this usually comes at the expense of their bottom line. However, CAVA has shockingly shown that it can grow without cutting too much into earnings. CAVA’s restaurant-level profit margin for the third quarter of 2024 was 25.6%, representing a 41.9% increase from the third quarter of 2023. For comparison, Chipotle’s restaurant-level profit margin for the third quarter of 2024 was 25.5%. While CAVA is only slightly above this mark, it is also a much less mature company than CMG, which makes this feat extraordinary.

20-Year DCF Analysis

One of the most effective ways to value CAVA is by conducting a DCF analysis using comparable metrics from Chipotle’s early years, starting in 2004 when Chipotle had a similar store count (298 stores at the end of 2003 compared to CAVA’s 309 stores at the end of 2023) and comparable growth rates. In this model, we applied highly favorable assumptions, giving CAVA the benefit of the doubt that it could evolve into the next Chipotle. We believe the result of this DCF model reflects a very bullish case for CAVA’s stock.

While a 10-year DCF might be the norm, a 20-year model is better suited for CAVA. Let’s go over our reasonings and assumptions one by one, followed by valuation results and interpretations:

  • Why 20 Years?

    • Every company's growth eventually stabilizes, but we do not anticipate CAVA’s growth rate flattening or dropping below 5% within the next 10 years. For comparison, Chipotle’s revenue CAGR from 2004 to 2023 was 17.37%. Given this, a 10-year DCF analysis would likely underestimate CAVA’s long-term growth potential.

  • Revenue Growth

    • We assume a 16.17% CAGR for CAVA from 2024 to 2044, which we consider the most bullish scenario. This projection represents 93% of Chipotle’s revenue CAGR over a similar period, reflecting our optimistic outlook on CAVA’s growth potential. We realize that Chipotle’s salmonella incidents derailed its growth from 2015 to 2016, but its recent resurgence under Brian Niccol after 2018 was also not to be dismissed. All in, we see a 16% revenue CAGR as tremendously generous for CAVA. This also matches CAVA’s goal of opening 1,000 stores by 2032.

  • EBIT Growth and Margin
    • We are assuming the EBIT margin to grow from 6% in 2024 to 15% by 2028, after which slowly expand to 20% in 2044. This is an extremely bullish assumption as CMG's current operating profit margin stands at around 17% and has never topped 18% since going IPO. Such EBIT projection will result in a 23% CAGR in EBIT from 2024 to 2044, even topping CMG's EBIT CAGR during its growth years.

  • Taxes

    • We assumed a 28% tax rate for CAVA. Taxes and regulations are unpredictable and fluctuate, but we think a 28% tax rate is appropriate since this is the average tax rate for Chipotle over the last 20 years.

  • Depreciation & Amortization

    • D&A is 5% of revenue during the initial years and gradually down to 3.5% of revenue in the final years.

  • Changes in NWC

    • This is 5% of revenue during the initial years and gradually down to 3% of revenue in the final years.

  • Capital Expenditure

    • This is 11% of revenue during the initial years and gradually down to 5% of revenue in the final years.

  • Terminal Growth Rate

    • We assumed a 6% growth rate in free cash flow in perpetuity and +/- 1% in our sensitivity analysis.

  • WACC

    • We assumed a WACC of 10% for CAVA and +/- 2% in our sensitivity analysis. The discount rate is one of the important aspects of a DCF model. With the 10-year UST currently yielding ~4.5%, and CAVA being a high beta stock, 10% is more than appropriate in our opinion.

DCF Fair Value

With the abovementioned bullish inputs, our DCF analysis suggests an enterprise value of $10.6B, or $93/share based on the current shares outstanding of 114 million shares.

After sensitizing the WACC and Terminal Growth Rate assumptions, the valuation range expands drastically with share price estimates between $46 to $423/share. Of course, such a dramatic price range is not to be taken too seriously as it includes outlier scenarios with very little probability. Rather, a more likely price range with rational sensitivity analysis is between $60 to $191.

At the high-end price of $191, this is assuming the most bullish case not only at the company level, but also at the macroeconomic level. CAVA would have to outgrow Chipotle's impressive growth rates from 2004-2023, and achieve operating margins that Chipotle has never accomplished over the course of its company history, in the next two decades and more, combined with a lower interest rate environment. Frankly, we don't see that happening, and we strongly believe the stock is very much overvalued at its current price level.

The lower-end price of $60 assumes a slightly challenging environment with an 11% WACC and a terminal growth rate of just 5%. At this price, we believe CAVA is worth consideration for investment. Don't forget, this price still assumes bullish growth and profitability from 2025 to 2044.

Customer Experience and Why CAVA Won't Be The Next Chipotle

As a team that has visited CAVA and experienced all that it has to offer, we were certainly impressed with the visual appeal. From the moment we walked into the store, we could already smell the fresh ingredients used in their food offerings. The interior had a very rustic look, unlike anything we had ever seen within a fast-casual chain setting. As for the kitchen and cafeteria-style serving table, it reminded us quite a bit of Chipotle, albeit with a completely different cuisine and a greater variety of choices. However, the kitchen appeared much more open and visible to customers, indicating a commitment to transparency and food safety— a problem that Chipotle has had in the past.

In terms of the food itself, we thought it was great. Many of us had never really experienced Mediterranean-style cuisine before, and it was delicious. For a fast-casual dining chain, the meals left us feeling good and refreshed. The prices, however, are noticeably higher than comparable fast-casual restaurants, with portions not necessarily matching the cost. This raises the question of whether CAVA could ever attract lower-income customers as Chipotle did. We think not.

As for customer service, it seemed adequate for the most part. One particularly important thing we noticed was a sign indicating that first responders, following the Los Angeles wildfires, would be able to receive a free meal at CAVA—demonstrating their support for the surrounding community. Additionally, something that caught our attention upon entering was the clearly advertised loyalty program, which was enticing for customers to join. The employees themselves were engaging and interactive, but one downside to the experience was the rather slow operations. The amount of sauces, dressings, and hummus toppings is quite overwhelming. This raises the question of whether CAVA can run its restaurants as efficiently as Chipotle and whether its model could ever work in a drive-thru setting.

Overall, in terms of pure restaurant experience—aside from financials and growth prospects—CAVA has tremendous upside potential, with a few areas that could be improved.

Conclusion

We thought highly of CAVA as a young and upcoming fast-casual restaurant. In the long run, we have little doubt that it will do very well. However, too much optimism has been priced into the stock's current valuation. From our DCF model, even under the most bullish assumptions, one might be able to argue that CAVA is fully priced. However, any less-than-perfect scenario, whether company-specific or macro-related, would easily drag the stock to the downside by 30-40%.

data.title: Encouraging Growth In Key Metrics Drives 55% Gain Year-To-Date For Cloudflare Stock
data.description:
Cloudflare’s (NYSE:NET) stock rose 20% in the days following Q4 earnings with strong momentum for its Workers AI platform and accelerations in multiple key metrics, paving the way for the stock to potentially reaccelerate revenue growth while driving a shift to GAAP profitability. Management also expressed a high degree of optimism for AI inference and growth opportunities through 2025, especially for its Workers AI platform.

I break down the growth in the most important key metrics, Workers AI momentum and commentary about AI opportunities below.

Workers Developers Grew from 2 Million to 3 Million in 2024

Cloudflare has quietly amassed a large developer base with tens of millions of developers, allowing for a rather frictionless ramp for its Workers AI platform, with 2024 seeing the platform record more than 50% growth. Workers AI positions Cloudflare to capitalize on the growing AI inference market via its unique approach that offers performance at or exceeding hyperscalers’ at a lower cost.

In Q4, management noted that active Workers developers crossed 3 million, marking a 50% growth from 2 million active developers in Q1 and a 25% growth from 2.4 million developers in Q2. Since November 2023, active Workers developers have risen more than 200%.

The Workers platform essentially is Cloudflare’s moat and what separates it from the rest of software in terms of what it can provide for AI. With Workers, Cloudflare eliminates cold starts, allowing code to be executed the moment it's received – we had explained to our premium members in 2023 that it's a "combination of competing with the hyperscalers, delivering app performance at faster speeds... while keeping prices low... that's unique to Cloudflare," and what will help the platform drive growth down the road.

Management hammered home the significance of Workers in Q4’s earnings call, emphasizing that the:

Killer application for Cloudflare Workers is turning out to be AI. The model of programming is uniquely suited for building tools like AI agents, and our serverless architecture, which allows you to pay only for what you use based on CPU or GPU type, positions Workers to become the go-to platform for developers who want the best price performance for AI inference and agentic workflows.”

Cloudflare is now capitalizing on the developer moat that it has built with Workers over the past few years, as developers continue to choose the platform due to the unparalleled efficiencies and cost advantages it can offer when it comes to AI inference. Customers executing inference tasks could pay up to 250% more with hyperscalers than with Cloudflare’s pay-per-use model, per management.

Workers also saw significant deal momentum in the fourth quarter. Cloudflare reported its largest customer win in Q4, a $20 million contract with a Fortune 1000 tech firm, that includes Workers and application security, while a leading AI firm expanded with a $13.5 million deal for services including Workers, R2, and more.

What could easily be overlooked (and what arguably is very important) was management’s discussion on GPU utilization rates. Management explained that while a typical customer at a hyperscaler “is seeing sub-10% utilization across their GPUs, our peak utilization of GPUs is now around 70%,” though troughs are much lower with room to improve in 2025. What this means is that Cloudflare “can get effectively 7 times today the amount of work out of $1 of capex spent,” allowing them to capture the excess either in margins or pass it on to customers via lower costs. Improving troughs in utilization should theoretically lead to faster processing times and an ability to handle more requests for customers, all while doing so for cheaper and at a higher margin.

Multiple Key Metrics Support Confidence in Cloudflare’s Revenue Reacceleration

Though Workers growth and commentary on improving GPU utilization rates were quiet drivers of the report, multiple key metrics showed growth in Q4, supporting management’s confidence that the first half of 2025 would mark a turning point with the strength of its business accelerating in the second half.

Cloudflare guided for 23.7% growth in Q1 to $468-469 million in revenue, and that's currently forecast to be the bottom for the year, with revenue expected to reaccelerate to the 27% level exiting the year. All it would take is a few beat-and-raise quarters to put Cloudflare on track to surpass the 30% growth level by the end of 2025.

Multiple key metrics showed strong growth in Q4, with a handful of analysts praising the productivity strides and strong execution on these key metrics in the quarter:

  • Acceleration in growth in >$1M customers
  • High concentration of net new adds for >$1M customers in Q4
  • Acceleration in paying customers
  • Acceleration in billings and strong Pool of Funds activity
  • DBNRR expanding

Cloudflare’s Paying Customer Growth Accelerates

Cloudflare reported 173 customers in the >$1M cohort at the end of 2024, for 47% YoY growth, up from 37% growth last year. Management noted that of the 55 of the net new adds in 2024, more than half came in Q4, implying at least 28 in the quarter, and 27 combined for the other three quarters; a significant uptick.

Paying customers increased 25% YoY to 237,714 at the end of Q4, an 8-point acceleration from the start of the year. From late 2022 through 2023, growth had remained in the mid-teens, with the recent uptick in paying customers coming alongside growth in AI applications and more mainstream AI usage, with numerous consumer-facing and enterprise-focused models being released. What’s impressive is that Cloudflare is matching 2022 growth rates at a nearly 100,000 larger paying customer scale. Additionally, two of the past three quarters have seen paying customers grow at 7% QoQ, suggesting that a further acceleration could be still in store.

Cloudflare’s Billings Growth Tops 30% Again

Billings meaningfully inflected in Q4, rising nearly 32% YoY and 22% QoQ to $548 million. Growth had previously been in the low-20% range for the first three quarters of 2024. Billings likely benefited from a “notable uptick in close rates” and “an improvement in sales cycles,” with management mentioning that the majority of large customers “whose deals had slipped from Q3, reengaged and signed significant contracts in Q4.”

Pool of Funds activity was quite strong as well, with activity comparable to Q3 at around 9 percentage points. Management noted that a $20 million Pool of Funds contract with a Fortune 100 tech company was the largest new customer win in company history, along with a $13.5 million deal with a leading AI firm. Pool of Funds is expected to pick up strength in the second half of the year after leading to some near-term headwinds as customers transition to these contracts.

There were other encouraging signs within ramped account executives, with management saying that they:

Delivered a 10 percentage point increase in ramped AEs achieving over 80% of quota compared with 2023, with most gains coming in the 125% or higher attainment cohorts.” For 2025, management is expecting YoY growth in ramped account executives to “accelerate each quarter throughout 2025, further laying the foundation for Cloudflare's next phase of growth at scale.”

DBNRR Ticks Higher to 111%

Cloudflare reported that its dollar-based net retention rate (DBNRR) ticked 1 point higher to 111%, marking the first time the metric has grown since Q3 2023. Management commented that:

There can be some variability in this metric quarter-to-quarter, but we believe the recent decelerating trend in DNR is stabilizing despite continued near-term headwinds from increased traction with pool of fund contracts.

Cloudflare’s GPU Investments Rising

With the accelerations in billings, paying customers and strong net adds in its largest customer cohort, Cloudflare laid the foundation for increased investments to support growth through 2025. While this is likely to be slightly detrimental to margins, with management guided for an adjusted operating margin of 11.6% in Q1 versus 14.6% in Q2, it paves the way for Cloudflare to meet higher demand and reinvigorate revenue growth.

Management said that the “accelerating shift from AI training to AI inference has given us confidence to continue to increase our investment in our GPU rollout as we provision greater capacity to support demand in 2025.” As a result, network capex is expected to be 12-13% of revenue in 2025, up from 10% in 2024.

As has been the case with hyperscalers, who had been quite vocal over the past few quarters that demand regularly outpaced GPU supply, Cloudflare signaled that its investments in GPU capacity would go toward supporting higher demand as workloads shift toward AI inference.

Cloudflare is Inching Closer to GAAP Profitability

Breaking into GAAP profitability on the bottom line is a "holy grail" for cloud stocks in part due to high SBC levels that push operating expenses above 100% of revenue. Cloudflare is inching closer to reaching GAAP profitability on the bottom, though GAAP operating income is a bit further away.

Cloudflare reported a GAAP operating loss of ($34.7 million), or a GAAP operating margin of -7.5% in Q4. Though this declined slightly from -7.2% in Q3 due to some headwinds to gross margin, it marked a 7-point improvement from the -14.4% operating margin in Q1.

Cloudflare is showing prudent cost management, bringing expenses down from 92% of revenue in Q1 to below 84% of revenue in Q4. R&D expenses ticked slightly higher sequentially in Q4, understandable given the investments Cloudflare is making in its network, while marketing expenses declined 1.4 points to 41.7% of revenue.

GAAP net margin is making strides toward positive territory, with Cloudflare benefiting from net interest income and improvements to operating margin. GAAP net margin improved from -9.4% in Q1 to -2.8% in Q4, or a loss of just ($12.8 million). This improvement comes despite SBC remaining elevated at just above 20% of revenue.

Cloudflare is currently not expected to break into GAAP profitability this year, with analysts expecting a loss each quarter of 2025. However, the improvements in operating margin from cost management efforts and a potential revenue reacceleration could shift that story. Other best-of-breed cloud names such as CrowdStrike and DataDog have seen strong returns after breaking into GAAP profitability on the bottom line.

Cloudflare’s Valuation Looks Elevated

Even though management is quite confident in the AI inference growth opportunities in 2025, there’s not yet tangible evidence of a reacceleration on the top line, and Cloudflare is trading at its highest valuation in more than two years. Key metrics are showing underlying signs that support revenue reaccelerating, but the valuation amplifies risks to the downside until the acceleration story materializes.

On the top line, Cloudflare is trading at its highest valuations since May 2022, at 28x forward revenue, above where it had previously found resistance at 22x to 23x forward revenue. This makes it one of the most expensive cloud stocks aside from Palantir, trading at a near 100% premium to DataDog and Snowflake, despite all three reporting revenue growth in the high 26% to 28% range for the most recent quarter.

On a free cash flow basis, Cloudflare is the most expensive of the four here, trading at 350x free cash flow versus 250x for Palantir and 75x to 77x for Snowflake and DataDog. Free cash flow has hovered at ~10% of revenue for 2024, below Snowflake at 20% through fiscal Q3 and DataDog at 29% for 2024 due to Cloudflare’s much higher investments in its infrastructure – Cloudflare spent $185 million in 2024, vs. $35 million for DataDog in 2024 and $34 million for Snowflake (through Q3).

For adjusted EPS, Cloudflare is valued at 217x forward earnings of $0.80, vs. 210x for Palantir and 186x for Snowflake. This is a significant re-rating in a short period of time, with Cloudflare being valued at half of this multiple in October, at 105x forward earnings. Adjusted earnings growth is also expected to be minimal in 2025, with management guiding for barely 6% growth to $0.79 to $0.80 this year.

Conclusion

The Workers platform exhibited strong momentum through 2024 with active developers rising 50% to surpass 3 million, widening Cloudflare’s developer moat as it aims to harness growth in AI via its positioning at the edge and ability to offer high-performance, low-cost inference. A handful of key metrics inflected and accelerated in unison in Q4, a positive sign for growth heading into 2025.

Cloudflare’s management expressed confidence for 2025 on AI inference-driven opportunities, with multiple key metrics suggesting that a revenue reacceleration could be in store. Given the soft Q1 and fiscal 2025 guide and elevated valuation, Cloudflare is in a spot where it has to prove that it can meaningfully inflect revenue growth based on the underlying strength in large customer deals, billings, and customer growth. We think the stock will prove this; yet we also book gains at specific price levels to reduce risk.

Damien Robbins, Equity Analyst for the Tech Insider Network, contributed to this analysis.

Disclaimer: This is not financial advice. Please consult with your financial advisor in regards to any stocks you buy.

Recommended Reading:

Palantir Stock Sets Path Towards 40% Growth

DeepSeek Creates Buying Opportunity For Nvidia Stock

5 Top Tech Stocks Of 2024: Year In Review

This Is Not Broadcom's 'Nvidia Moment' Yet

data.title: Gold Eyes Fresh Highs, U.S.-Russia Talks Fail To Quell Haven Demand
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Risk aversion persisted in the markets today, even as a U.S.-Russia meeting in Saudi Arabia sparked hopes for a potential decline in safe-haven demand. Additionally, reports emerged of a phase two agreement being reached between Israel and Hamas.

Both developments carried the expectation of easing the geopolitical tensions that have weighed on global sentiment over the past 12 to 18 months. However, these events have so far failed to significantly alleviate the sense of geopolitical risk, keeping haven demand firmly in place.

US-Russia Talks in Saudi Arabia

A US and Russian delegation met in Saudi Arabia today to discuss a potential end to the Ukraine conflict. Market participants may have been eyeing a drop in geopolitics, which may lead to a drop in Gold prices as haven demand falls. However, this failed to materialize despite reports that the talks were positive.

Key Quotes and Reports from the Meeting from Both US and Russian Officials:

-US, Russia Agree To Appoint Teams To End Ukraine Conflict – US Official

-US Spox Bruce: One Call One Meeting Not Enough For Enduring Peace

-Russia’s Dmitriev: Russian, US Officials Have Separate Discussion On Future Economic Cooperation, Including Global Energy Prices – RTRS

-US, Russia Peace Proposal Would Include Ukraine Elections – Fox

-US State Sec Rubio: US, Russia Agree To Restore Embassy Staffing -AP

-U.S. National Security Adviser Waltz: There Will Be Talks About Territory And Security Guarantees

-US Sec Of State Rubio: Everyone Involved In Conflict Has To Be Part Of Talks

-US Sec Of State Rubio: European Union Needs To Be Involved At Some Point

-US National Security Adviser Waltz: US Allies Are Being Consulted On Ukraine

-US National Security Adviser Waltz: US Is Glad Europe Is Talking About Contributing More Forcefully To Ukraine Security

-Russia’s ForMin Lavrov: We Agreed To Form Process For Ukraine Conflict Settlement

-Russia’s ForMin Lavrov: There Were Also High Interest To Lift Barriers For Economic Cooperation

-Russia’s ForMin Lavrov: European Troops In Ukraine Is Unacceptable For Russia

-Russia’s ForMin Lavrov: I Can Say That Today’s Talks Were Not Unsuccessful

Despite the comments by Russia’s Foreign Minister Lavrov that the talks were not unsuccessful, the road ahead may be a long and bumpy one. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy announced that his visit to Saudi Arabia has been rescheduled for March 10th, as he awaits the arrival of the U.S. delegation in Kyiv. This could explain why markets have not had any meaningful reaction as of yet.

Israel-Hamas Phase Two Deal Appears on Course

In another positive for risk sentiment, both sides appear ready to discuss phase two of the ceasefire. Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar stated that negotiations for the second phase of the hostage deal will commence soon. This view was echoed by Hamas Gaza Chief Hayya, who announced that the group is prepared to begin immediate negotiations for the second phase of the Gaza ceasefire agreement.

Another potential positive for geopolitical risk. Israeli Foreign Minister meanwhile acknowledged he is aware that Arab states are coming up with a plan for Gaza as Egyptian President Sisi is set to travel to Saudi Arabia for discussions. On the flip side however, the Israeli Foreign Minister has said they are unwilling to accept or support a plan that would see civilian control of Gaza transferred from Hamas to the Palestinian Authority.

Tariff Developments and the Week Ahead

The Dollar has so far had a limited impact on Gold prices. We have seen spikes when key data has missed estimates but the precious metal quickly recovers. This is a sign that haven demand and risk sentiment remains skewed in favor of further gains for Gold prices.

Tariffs by the US have heated up as well which continues to support Gold prices. President Trump has said that tariffs on automobiles would come on April 2, while Chinese lithium company Jiangsu Jiuwu Hi-Tech halted exports of processing equipment per LSEG.

Signs that a global trade war remains a massive concern for market participants moving forward.

On the data front, the FOMC minutes will be out on Wednesday followed by S&P PMI data on Friday. Both of these events could lead to a spike in volatility, but I do expect any moves to prove short-lived.

Technical Analysis – Gold (XAU/USD)

From a technical analysis standpoint, this analysis is a follow up from the technicals last week. Read: Gold (XAU/USD) Outlook: $3000/oz Target Possible as Safe Haven Demand Rises

Gold prices did experience a pullback last week following hotter than expected inflation data from the US. However, this proved short-lived as prices retested its alltime highs around the 2942/0z handle.

On the daily timeframe, the RSI period 14 is back in overbought territory. As we know though, an instrument can remain in overbought territory for extended periods of time.

Gold (XAU/USD) Daily Chart, February 18, 2025

Dropping down to a four-hour chart H4, markets are in a sort of range with highs around 2937 and lows around 2881.

A rejection at 3937 could lead to a retest of support at 2924 and 2913 before the 2900 comes into focus.

On the upside, a break of 2927 and the all-time high at 2942 will see focus shift toward the 2950 and 2975 resistance handles.

Gold (XAU/USD) Four-Hour H4 Chart, February 18, 2025

Support

  • 2924
  • 2013
  • 2900

Resistance

  • 2937
  • 2942
  • 2950

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: REIT Earnings Halftime Report
data.description:
REIT Earnings Halftime Report

We're at the halfway point of another consequential real estate earnings season, with 60 of the roughly 150 equity REITs and 18 of the 38 mortgage REITs representing 50% of the total market capitalization now having reported results. Buoyed by much-needed relief on the interest rate front, REIT earnings season is off to a strong start both fundamentally and performance-wise. Of the 51 equity REITs that provide full-year guidance for Funds from Operations ("FFO"), 31 (61%) beat their most recent full-year outlook, while just three (6%) missed their outlook - above the historical average "beat rate" for the fourth quarter of roughly 55%. Strong trends within the real estate sector come alongside a similarly solid start to earnings season across the broader U.S. equity market. FactSet reports that 76% of the 385 S&P 500 constituents that have reported results beat consensus Earnings Per Share ("EPS") estimates, which is slightly above the 10-year average of 75%.

Overarching trends have generally been consistent with the "green shoots" identified in our REIT Earnings Preview. As expected, we've observed a post-election rebound in logistics demand, a continued recovery of office leasing, and sluggish residential rent growth amid peaking multifamily supply. Healthcare REITs have been the "star of the show" so far this earnings season, as senior housing results showed robust - and continuing - momentum in senior housing operating fundamentals. Retail REITs have also been upside surprises thus far, with strip center and mall REITs reporting another quarter of near-record occupancy rates and rent growth, while pushing back on concern over a sudden surge in retail bankruptcies. Results from Net Lease and Residential Mortgage REITs - two of the most rate-sensitive sectors - have been surprisingly steady despite the turbulence in late 2024. Office REIT results have been mildly disappointing, as an encouraging property-level recovery has been stymied by higher financing costs. Relatedly, Commercial Mortgage REITs have disappointed so far after another flurry of office loan defaults and a stubborn uptick in sector-wide delinquency rates, underscoring the bifurcation between property-level trends and bottom-line profitability.

We've seen 20 REITs raise their dividends since the start of earnings season - slightly ahead of last year's pacing at this time, boosted by the first dividend hikes in nearly a decade for a pair of major healthcare REITs: Senior housing REIT Ventas (VTR) raised its dividend by 7% (2.9% dividend yield) - its first dividend hike since 2018. Medical office REIT Healthpeak (DOC) raised its dividend by 2% (6.2% dividend yield) - its first dividend increase since 2016. Other highlights include a hearty 20% increase from First Industrial (FR), a 15% increase from single-family rental REIT American Homes (AMH), a 12% increase from strip center REIT Urban Edge (UE), a pair of 10% hikes from Equinix (EQIX) and Iron Mountain (IRM), and a healthy 8% dividend hike from manufactured housing REIT Equity Lifestyle (ELS). That said, we also saw the first REIT dividend reduction of the year last week, with commercial lender Ares Commercial (ACRE) slashing its payout by 40% after reporting ongoing distress in its office and life sciences-backed loans. Below, we award each property sector a Halftime Grade - ranked in order from best to worst - and highlight quick incremental positives and negatives we've observed.

Healthcare REIT Halftime Report

Healthcare: (Halftime Grade: A-)

  • Positives: Stellar senior housing trends continue in 2025, healthier skilled nursing operators, VTR & DOC dividend increases.
  • Negatives: Status quo for medical office, lethargic lab space fundamentals, lingering SNF policy uncertainty

Regional Mall Earnings

Mall: (Halftime Grade: B+)

  • Positives: Strong leasing despite retailer bankruptcies, Simon's dividend hike to pre-pandemic levels, positive FFO outlook for 2025
  • Negatives: Flat tenant sales & foot traffic, Concern on spending health of European & lower-end American consumer

Net Lease REIT Halftime Report

Net Lease: (Halftime Grade: B+)

  • Positives: Perfect slate of FFO beats, healthy but disciplined acquisition activity, WPC's return to growth, positive commentary on retail credit
  • Negatives: Modest cap rate compression, additional retail categories join the watch list (car washes, following Zips bankruptcy)

Data Center REIT Halftime Report

Data Center: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: EQIX's record bookings in Q4, DLR's long-awaited return to FFO growth, upbeat DeepSeek and AI commentary
  • Negatives: Softer bookings for DLR, moderating (but still solid) pricing power after record 2024, IRM's disappointing 2025 bookings outlook

Strip Center REIT Halftime Report

Strip Centers: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: Solid 2025 outlook implying FFO growth near 5%, little slowdown in leasing activity, Renewal spreads at fresh record-highs
  • Negatives: Uptick in tenant bankruptcies, less bullish retail commentary, some "mean-reversion" from KRG after sector-leading pandemic growth

Industrial REIT Halftime Report

Industrial: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: Post-election demand rebound, encouraging 2025 outlook for moderating supply growth by mid-year, strength in Sunbelt markets
  • Negatives: Ongoing weakness in Southern California, market rents remain negative, moderating rent spreads after record-setting increases

Office REIT Halftime Report

Office: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: Leasing activity recovers to pre-pandemic average, Sunbelt & NYC strength, CUZ remains "unicorn" among office REITs
  • Negatives: Disappointing 2025 FFO outlook on higher interest rate expense (BDN in particular), DOGE concerns hit government office

Apartment REIT Halftime Report

Apartment: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: Buoyant renewal rent growth, record-low turnover helping to control expenses, healthy occupancy despite record supply growth
  • Negatives: Weak new lease rates amid peaking multifamily supply, FFO growth resumes in 2026, limited appetite for external growth

Mortgage REIT Halftime Report

Residential mREITs: (Halftime Grade: B+)

  • Positives: Decent results given harsh macro backdrop, positive dividend commentary, upbeat outlook on return on equity potential
  • Negatives: Results from CIM underscore the potential Book Value impact of volatile interest rates (but no permanent damage in this case)

Commercial mREITs: (Halftime Grade: C+)

  • Positives: Multifamily improvement, BXMT's "positive inflection" and improved loan performance, limited issues outside of office
  • Negatives: Poor outcomes on office resolutions, ACRE's additional office write-off and life science downgrade, GPMT's student housing downgrade

Previewing The Second-Half of Earnings

Buoyed by much-needed relief on the interest rate front, REIT earnings season is off to a strong start both fundamentally and performance-wise - but there's still much to uncover, with results still outstanding from the entire hotel, self-storage, billboard, cannabis, farmland, and cold storage sectors, along with the majority of smaller-cap REITs and several "embattled" names including hospital owner Medical Properties Trust (MPW). As discussed above, Senior Housing REITs have been the "star of the show" so far this earnings season, while Retail REITs have also been upside surprises. Commercial Mortgage REITs have disappointed so far, underscoring the bifurcation between property-level trends and bottom-line profitability resulting from financing challenges. We'll continue to provide real-time coverage and follow-up analysis articles in our Daily Recap and Weekly Outlook reports.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Center, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.

Disclosure: Hoya Capital Real Estate advises two Exchange-Traded Funds listed on the NYSE. In addition to any long positions listed below, Hoya Capital is long all components in the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index and in the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index. Index definitions and a complete list of holdings are available on our website.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Momentum Leaves Other Equity Factors In The Dust So Far In 2025
data.description:
The momentum factor is crushing the competition year to date, based on a set of ETF proxies through Friday’s close (Feb. 14). After a strong run last year, momentum is roaring higher in 2025 in relative and absolute terms.

The iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF (MTUM) has surged 10.3% this year, more than twice the gain for the broad equity market benchmark via SPDR S&P 500 (SPY). The next-best factor is also well behind MTUM’s bull run so far in 2025: An ETF tracking the high-beta factor (SPHB) is in a distant second-place with a 5.4% rise this year.

Momentum’s strong leadership is all the more striking for a year so far that reflects a rising tide that’s lifted all factor boats. But while everything is posting gains, the laggards are far behind the leaders. The weakest performer so far this year: small-cap value (IJS), which is barely hanging on to a razor-thin advance of just 0.3% year to date.

What’s driving MTUM’s bull run? The fund’s top-two holdings are a key part of its success so far this year. MTUM’s top holding – semiconductor firm Broadcom (AVGO), with a 6.2% portfolio weight, according Morningstar.com - is up 16% so far in 2025. The second-largest holding is Walmart (WMT) with a 5.4% weight and a 15.2% year to date gain.

By comparison, the two largest holdings in the market benchmark – SPDR S&P 500 (SPY) – are posting sharply softer results: Apple (AAPL) is down 2.2% year to date while Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) is up a modest 3.4%.

The momentum factor, in short, is killing it so far in 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
